Scenario.overview: Healthcare Sector Complex The Night Dragon campaign, conducted by expert attackers, aims to infiltrate healthcare organizations. They employ sophisticated methods including extensive reconnaissance, development of resources, multiple vectors of initial access, and a multi-stage persistence strategy to achieve their objectives, ultimately leading to data exfiltration and impact operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Command & Control Server, User Account, Public IP/Network Block, Network Traffic, File
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: General Access Method, Web Shell, User Account Authentication, Disabling Security Tools, Mimikatz Tool, Network Service Scanning, Network Traffic Flow, File Access, Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, File Modification

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities mapping network nodes, hosts, devices, and routes to gather network ranges, IP addresses, and other information to identify potential targets within the healthcare sector.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The attackers establish servers and email accounts for command and control (C2) and phishing campaigns, setting up the necessary infrastructure for their operations.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Specific individuals within the organization are targeted using tailored emails with malicious attachments designed to gain initial access.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 121 Name: Exploit Non-Production Interfaces
stage.description: Attackers identify and exploit vulnerabilities in public-facing healthcare applications or services to gain initial access.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: A web shell is installed on compromised servers to maintain persistent access to the environment.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645 Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used to escalate privileges within the system.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: Protection mechanisms like antivirus software are impaired to avoid detection.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Login and password hashes are extracted from the operating systems.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers map the internal network to find valuable data and vulnerable systems.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 324 Name: TCP (ISN) Sequence Predictability Probe
stage.description: Remote service sessions are used to move laterally through the network.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 328 Name: TCP 'RST' Flag Checksum Probe
stage.description: Attacks search for and collect sensitive patient data, research, and intellectual property from local systems.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: Collected data is transferred to attacker-controlled servers using established C2 channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 675 Name: Retrieve Data from Decommissioned Devices
stage.description: Backups and critical systems are destroyed to prevent recovery and cause maximum disruption.

Scenario.summary: The Night Dragon campaign employs a complex multistage approach targeting healthcare organizations. Starting with extensive reconnaissance, they progress to establishing command and control infrastructure, gaining initial access via spearphishing, executing payloads, and ensuring persistence. They then escalate privileges, evade defenses, access credentials, discover valuable assets, move laterally, collect sensitive data, exfiltrate it over C2 channels, and finally impact the organization by destroying data.
